[Page 2]
METHODOLOGY :
The proposed approach begins with data collection
, where the dataset is loaded from a CSV file and the relevant input features (A, B) along with the target variable are selected. After this, feature engineering is performed to improve the model’s ability to capture complex relationships. New features such as interaction (A×BA \times BA×B) and polynomial terms (A2A^2A2, B2B^2B2) are created, increasing the feature space from 2 to 5 dimensions. Next, the dataset is divided into training and testing sets (80–20 split) to evaluate model performance on unseen data. The training data is further split into a validation set
, which is used to monitor the model during training and prevent overfitting. Before training, all features and target values are normalized using StandardScaler
, ensuring that the model trains efficiently and avoids issues due to different value ranges. A compact feedforward neural network is then designed with architecture (5 → 6 → 3 → 1). The model uses Swish activation function in hidden layers and He initialization for better weight initialization. During training, the model uses the Huber loss function
, which is robust to outliers by combining the advantages of Mean Squared Error and Mean Absolute Error. The model parameters are optimized using the Adam optimizer
, along with a cosine learning rate schedule to improve convergence. Training is performed in mini-batches, and early stopping is applied based on validation loss to stop training when no further improvement is observed, thus avoiding overfitting. After training, the best model weights are selected and used for prediction on the test dataset. The model is evaluated using standard metrics such as R² score, Mean Squared Error (MSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
. Additionally, a custom efficiency metric (Z) is calculated, which considers both prediction error and model size (number of parameters), ensuring that the model is not only accurate but also computationally efficient. Overall, this methodology combines feature engineering, efficient model design, robust training, and performance evaluation to achieve a balance between accuracy and model simplicity.
